coredata.dc:title: Application of chitosan as biocompatible polysaccharide in quantification of some benzodiazepines affecting sleep disorders: A new platform for preparation of bioactive scaffolds
coredata.dc:creator: Ashrafi, Hamideh
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Ansarin, Khalil
Ozkan, Sibel Ayşil
Jouyban, Abolghasem
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a reusable and time-saving strategy for the electrochemical polymerization of dopamine (EPD) in the presence of chitosan is reported. In this work, biocompatible polymer chitosan (CS) was electrodeposited on the surface of PDA modified glassy carbon electrode using layer-by-layer strategy. Owing to the abundant catechol and amine groups in the PDA layer, uniform gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) are deposited onto the POLY(DA-CS) and can effectively prevent the recombination of electron-hole pairs generated from photo-electrocatalysis and transfer the captured electrons to participate in the electrocatalytic reaction process. Compared with POLY(DA-CS), the as-prepared POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs organic-inorganic hybrid exhibit electrochemical sensitivity for the detection of some benzodiazepines and display excellent durability. Furthermore, the mussel-inspired electropolymerization strategy and the fast EPD-Au nanoparticle decorating process presented herein can be readily extended to various biomedical and pharmaceutical analysis. It is the adaptation of the established POLY(DA-CS)-Au NPs organic-inorganic hybrid for a selective, robust, and generalizable sensing system that is the emphasis of this work.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bioactive polysaccharide
Nanomaterials
Bioanalysis
Benzodiazepine
Sleep disorders
Drug metabolism

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work reports a part of PhD by research project of H. Ashrafi. We thank Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for supporting of this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) Repetitive CVs for GCE in the solution containing 0.005 g of dopamine in 5 mL of PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4). Scan rate is 100 mV/s. Number of cycle is 20. B) Repetitive CVs for Poly(DA)-GCE in the solution containing 0.002 g chitosan in 0.1 M HCl. Scan rate is 100 mV/s. Number of cycle is 20. C) Chronoamperometry of Poly(DA-CS) in the solution containing 5 mL HAuCl4·3H2O. E = 0 V, Time: 400 s.
FE-SEM images of Poly(DA) on the surface of GCE in different magnification.
FE-SEM images of Poly(CS) on the surface of poly(DA)/GCE (layer by layer) in different magnification.
FE-SEM images of Au NPs electrodeposited on the surface of Poly(DA-CS)-GCE in different magnification.
EDS of Poly(DA)-GCE (A), Poly(DA-CS)-GCE (B), Poly(DA-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE (C).
Typical CVs of GCE (A), POLY(DA)-GCE (B), POLY(DA-CS)-GCE (C), and POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE (D), in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 1 mM Clonazepam. Supporting electrolyte is 0.1 M PBS.
CVs of POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE the presence of selected benzodiazepines in different pH (2−10) of supporting electrolyte.

Typical CVs of PDA-CS-Au NPs-GCE in the presence of selected drugs in PBS (0.1 M) at the optimum pH with different scanning rates (from inside to outside): 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 70, 100, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 500, 550, 600, 650, 700,750, 800, 850, 900, 950 and 1000 mV/s. Inset: Variation of LnIp vs. Lnv.
Dependency of the peak potential versus Naperian logarithm of scan rates for the selected drugs using POLY(DA-CS)-Au NPs.
Stability of the prepared sensor in different cycle Number.

DPVs and SWVs of PDA-CS-AuNPs at various concentrations of selected drugs (0.01, 0.033, 0.06, 0.063, 0.066, 0.07, 0.075, 0.08, 0.085, 0.092, 0.1, 0.11, 0.12, 0.13, 0.19, 0.20, 0.24, 0.25, 0.27, 0.3, 0.33, 0.4, 0.42, 0.46, 0.5, 0.54, 0.6, 0.66, 0.75.0.8, 0.85, 1.1 and 1.39 μM). Pulse range: 0.05 V. Pulse width: 0.05 s. Pulse cycle: 0.2 s. Scanning speed: 5 mV/s. Supporting electrolyte is PBS (0.1 M) at optimum pH for each drug.
Calibration curves of selected drugs using POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE obtained by DPV technique.
Chemical structures of BDZs. Diazepam (DZ), Chlordiazepoxide (CDZ), Clonazepam (CLZ), and Oxazepam (OZ) and alprazolam (ALZ).
Electropolymerization of polydopamine.
Possible mechanism for electropolymerization of POLY(DA-CS) and electrodeposition of Au NPs.
The optimal pH for selected benzodiazepines using POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE in PBS (0.1 M).
Analytical parameters determined for calibration curves of selected drugs in the standard samples using POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE.
Comparison of the analytical results of this work, with the previous reports.

row.entry: Multiwall carbon nanotube-ionic liquid modified paste electrode
Lead film modified glassy carbon electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recoveries of selected cardiovascular drugs from spiked serum samples with POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of six determinations at optimum conditions. Values in the parentheses are standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Application of chitosan as biocompatible polysaccharide in quantification of some benzodiazepines affecting sleep disorders: A new platform for preparation of bioactive scaffolds

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Student Research Committee, Tuberculosis and Lung Disease Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tuberculosis and Lung Disease Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Ankara University, 06100 Ankara, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a reusable and time-saving strategy for the electrochemical polymerization of dopamine (EPD) in the presence of chitosan is reported. In this work, biocompatible polymer chitosan (CS) was electrodeposited on the surface of PDA modified glassy carbon electrode using layer-by-layer strategy. Owing to the abundant catechol and amine groups in the PDA layer, uniform gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) are deposited onto the POLY(DA-CS) and can effectively prevent the recombination of electron-hole pairs generated from photo-electrocatalysis and transfer the captured electrons to participate in the electrocatalytic reaction process. Compared with POLY(DA-CS), the as-prepared POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs organic-inorganic hybrid exhibit electrochemical sensitivity for the detection of some benzodiazepines and display excellent durability. Furthermore, the mussel-inspired electropolymerization strategy and the fast EPD-Au nanoparticle decorating process presented herein can be readily extended to various biomedical and pharmaceutical analysis. It is the adaptation of the established POLY(DA-CS)-Au NPs organic-inorganic hybrid for a selective, robust, and generalizable sensing system that is the emphasis of this work.

ce:section.ce:para: The treatment of sleep disorders has changed considerably in recent years [1]. Benzodiazepines are the most widely prescribed medicines to treat these diseases [2,3].
Benzodiazepines (BDZs) are psychoactive compounds with anticonvulsant, sedative, and hypnotic effects [4–6] and with important clinical usage [7].
These drugs are prescribed for patients with anxiety, sleep disorders, spasm, convulsive attacks and withdrawal symptoms [8–12].
The chemical structures of BDZs (diazepam, oxazepam, chlordiazepoxide, clonazepam and alprazolam) are shown in Scheme 1 .

Each drug has a therapeutic effect at a given dosages and has toxic effects at higher doses. In patients with impaired metabolism, such as abnormal kidney and liver function, obese, infants and old people, patients who it takes several medicines together and people who do not take the medication according to the order; drug dose is unreliable. In addition, some new drugs are toxic at a dosage slightly higher than the therapeutic dose. All of these factors have led to that dosage adjustment can be an important task for the clinical laboratory [13–16].

The determination of benzodiazepines has been an area of interest for chemists since the early 1960s, when this type of pharmaceutical compounds came on the market. Due to the importance of the quantitative determinations of benzodiazepines in pharmaceutical formulations and biological fluids several analytical methods have been developed. These methods include spectrophotometry, chromatography, immunoassays techniques and capillary electrophoresis (CE).

The spectrophotometer is easily affected by the dependent compounds. HPLC requires a high volume solution and large equipment and experienced operator. It is costly and time-consuming. The CE is easier than HPLC, but its repeatability is weak.

Taking into account the drawbacks of these methods, the relatively high costs of enzymes and antibodies, complicated instrumentation and time-consuming extraction steps, there is an interest in developing other sensitive techniques free from these problems.
Designing and developing an effective, inexpensive, simple, and efficient analysis method is an important topic in practical work.

Along with other methods, the electrochemical method is more important due to the advantages of fast response, cheaper device and less cost and not time-consuming, easy to work, and high sensitivity and specificity [17–25].

Electrochemical methods are practical and attractive because electrochemical instrumentation is usually compact, relatively inexpensive, reliable, and sensitive. The performance of the electrochemical sensors is based on the reaction with the analyte to produce the appropriate electrical signal related to concentration of analyte. Electrochemical sensors benefit from the advantages of easy to use, inexpensive and the ability to rapidly measure and analysis the concentration of analyte in complex samples, which have attracted much attention and application in the medical diagnosis [24]. Furthermore, the use of a wide range of nanomaterials, especially nanoparticles in analytical methods has many advantages and applications [25].

In the last two decades, the preparation of modified electrodes has attracted a lot of attention to the use of electroanalysis by the immobilization of electron mediators on the surface of electrodes. Recently, electropolymerization has received great attention because of its simplicity, selectivity and sensitivity towards analytes, strong adherence of polymer film to electrode surface and ability to provide larger surface area by forming homogeneous membrane [26]. Various interface based on polymers have been described in the literature for the development of some electrochemical sensors [27–33]. Moreover, the combination of these polymers with nanoparticles makes them attractive for the electrochemical analysis.

One of important polymers is poly dopamine (PDA). Most recently, a facile and effective one step electropolymerization method for the preparation of PDA-β-CD on the surface of GCE was developed in our research group [34].

After some of experimental study by our research group in this area [34], we found that the intensity of peak current obtained by PDA in electrochemical sensor is low. Also, stability of PDA on the surface of electrode is very small. Therefore, we need to use some of metals such as gold, iron, silver, and palladium to enhance the peak currents which is necessary for determination of trace amount of analytes in real samples. So, in the present study, electrochemically synthesized gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are used for enhancing the signal for electrochemical application. On the other hand, we need to use a biocompatible and stable compound for the increasing of electrode durability. Therefore, chitosan was selected as the best candidate for this purpose. Biocompatible material CS produced by treating shrimp and other crustacean shells with the alkali sodium hydroxide has been extensively used in sensor technology. Due to its attractive properties, that includes excellent film-forming ability, high permeability, good adhesion, nontoxicity, cheapness and a susceptibility to chemical modification. It also facilitates the electron transfer after its swelling in the reaction mixture due to its hydrophilic nature [35–37]. Besides, chitosan can be used as a dispersant to form stable composite which can form a stable film on the electrode surface [38].

Nanoparticles provide suitable substrates for bonding various biological molecules. Nanomaterials have been in the center of consideration due to electrical conductivity and high surface area, wide electrochemical substrate, excellent adhesion of molecules, chemical stability and electrochemical and physical properties. The polymer films used to modify electrodes significantly increase the electro-analytical capabilities, reaction rate and improve the stability of the electrode's reaction [39,40].

While the electrochemical synthesis of AuNPs is well established [41], the scheme presented herein enables particularly short deposition times, minimal solution preparation, and no requirement of an external linker or functionalizing ligand. Moreover, in-situ tailoring of the nanoparticle surface chemistry resulted in an improved affinity and selectivity. Therefore, poly(dopamine-chitosan) (PDA-CS) was used as an organic matrix for stabilization of AuNPs on the surface of GCE. So, the final nanocomposite was PDA-CS-Au NPs. This nanocomposite (PDA-CS-Au NPs) was used for electrochemical sensing of some benzodiazepines using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV).

In summary, these improved performances encouraged us to explore the possible leading role played by the presence of AuNPs on the structure of PDA-CS. The proposed nanocomposite (PDA-CS-AuNPs) increases the contact area with the analyte (some benzodiazepines), so they could increase the electrochemical active surface to interact with some benzodiazepines. Since the increase in geometric surface area is very important parameter in electrochemistry, therefore modification of PDA-CS with AuNPs can increase the rate of electrochemical reaction. Therefore, integration of AuNPs into PDA-CS provides an electrically conductive film towards increases Faradic currents. It was for these reasons that we decided to explore the possibility of using the PDA-CS-AuNPs as a platform for the development of electrochemical sensor towards quantification of some benzodiazepines. Finally, we investigated the fully electrochemical preparation of PDA-CS-AuNPs on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as a novel strategy for interface science. The formation of PDA-CS-AuNPs was firstly monitored. The latent activity of P-DA-CS -AuNPs coating towards detection and determination of some benzodiazepines was then investigated. The redox behavior of prepared nano-sensor was then characterized by CV and DPV. Then, we used PDA-CS-AuNPs as a new interface for electro-oxidation and determination of some benzodiazepines in human serum samples. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of the determination of some benzodiazepines based on their direct electrochemical oxidation on PDA-CS-AuNPs films.

All chemicals used in this work were of analytical reagent grade. Dopamine, chitosan, hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III)hydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), HCl, NaOH, were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All of drugs was purchurbed from Sobhan Daru Co. (Iran). Additional dilute solutions were prepared daily by accurate dilution just before use. Di‑potassium phosphate and mono‑potassium phosphate were obtained from Scharlau. Deionized water was purchased from Ghazi Pharmaceutical Co. (Tabriz, Iran). Stock standard solution of drugs (0.1 mM) was prepared by dissolving an appropriate mass of each drug in PBS buffer, and the daily standard working solutions of different concentrations were prepared by diluting the stock solution with buffer and were freshly used.

Electrochemical experiments were performed with a computer-controlled AUTOLAB system with PGSTAT302N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands), driven with NOVA 1.11 software. A conventional three-electrode cell was used with an Ag/AgCl (Methrom, The Netherlands) as a reference electrode, and a Pt wire was used as a counter-electrode. The working electrode was GCE (d = 2 mm) (from Azar electrode Co., Iran). For DPV measurements, a pulse width of 25 mV, a pulse time of 50 ms, and a scan rate of 50 mV/s were employed. The surface morphology of the modified electrodes was evaluated with a MIRA3 FE-SEM (SEM, Hitachi Ltd., Czech).

GCE (2 mm in diameter) was polished to a mirror-like finish with 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina slurry (Beuhler, USA) followed by rinsing thoroughly with double distilled water. Then it was successively sonicated in acetone and double distilled water, and allowed to dry at room temperature.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used to form the preparation thin film of poly(DA) on the surface of GCE. Fig. 1A presents 10 consecutively CV of GCE in the presence of 0.005 g dopamine containing 0.1 M PBS in the range of −1.0 to 1.0 V with scan rate of 100 mV/s. Also, for the preparation thin film of poly(DA) on the surface of GCE, homogenous dopamine (0.005 g) was added tom 5 mL PBS and transferred to electrochemical cell. Then poly(DA) films were electrodeposited onto GCE by CV at the scan rate of 100 mV/s for 15 cycles (Fig. 1A). As can be seen, the first positive scan, one oxidation peak at 0.23 V was observed, which is attributed to the oxidation of the DA. In the subsequent reversal scan, two reduction peaks were observed at +0.24 V and −0.428 V, which may be attributed to the reduction of dopaminequinone (DAQ) and dopaminechrome (DAC) as well as the intramolecular cyclization reaction product of dopaminequinone (DAQ). In the second positive scan, a new oxidation peak (a2) appeared at ca. 0.07 V, which may be attributed to the oxidation of leucodopaminechrome (LDAC). Simultaneously, the peak current of peak (a1) and peak (c1) decreased cycle by cycle, and the peak current of peak (a2) and peak (c2) continuously increased with continuous scanning, reflecting the continuous growth of the film. When the cyclic potential scan reached 15 cycles, the peak currents hardly changed. These facts indicate that DA was successfully polymerized on the surface of GCE by the electropolymerization method. The polymerization mechanism of DA is described as follows: dopaminechrome (DAC) molecules partly may evolve to the more stable 5,6‑dihydroindole molecules of two aromatic rings via isomerization, and 5,6‑indolequinone is then electropolymerized to form polymers [43]. Scheme 1 shows electropolymerization of POLY(DA) at the surface of GCE.

It has been confirmed that monomer containing NH2 can be electropolymerized at the surface of electrode to form a conducting film via covalent bond (CN) between electrode and NH2. DA has two NH2 and two NH groups and that can easily be oxidized to produce NH2. Covalent bond (CN) is then formed by the free radical reaction of NH2 with GCE surface with excellent stability. Therefore, the covalent bond (CN) might be formed between carbon electrode and the α-amino group of dopamine. It is important to point out that, during the high potential scan rate, the α-amino free radical will easily form and the polymerization of the poly amino acid film could be formed on the surface of GCE (Scheme 2 ).

At the second step for preparation of modified electrode, the three-electrode system was transferred into 0.1 M HCl containing 2 g/L CS, and 20 repetitive cycle in the potential range from −1 to 1 V vs. Ag/AgCl at a scan rate of 100 mV/s (Fig. 1B). Therefore, POLY(DA-CS)-GCE was prepared in this step. As we know, the carbocyclic functional group on POLY(DA) are beneficial to the combination with CS from the electrostatic point of view, since CS dissolved in acid (HCl in the present work) is positively charged. Therefore, at the final modification step for organic interface, CS was deposited on the surface of POLY(DA) from aqueous solution in the potential range from −1 to 1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl at a scan rate of 100 mV/s using cyclic voltammetry techniques (Fig. 1B). So, the results show that the hydrophilic CS can interact with POLY(DA) via CO2 − and NH3 + side groups (amid), which enable forming distinctive assembly network between the CS chains. Finally, thin film of POLY(DA-CS) was obtained at the surface of GCE (Scheme 3 ).

At the next step for preparation of conductive and durable interface, AuNPs were electrochemically synthesized at the surface POLY(DA-CS) modified GCE using chronoamperometric technique. The electrochemical solution consisted of 1.5 mM aqueous solution of HAuCl4·3H2O, while applied potential was 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl at room temperature (Fig. 1C). After deposition, the electrodes were washed with distilled water and dried in air. The modified electrode was denoted as POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE. Thus, synthesized nanocomposite is referred as POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE throughout the text.

The voltammograms of bare GCE, POLY(DA-CS)-GCE, POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE in the absence and presence of Clonazepam (as sample drug) were shown in Fig. 6 which the supporting electrolyte was 0.1 M PBS. As seen in Fig. 6A any oxidation signal was not exhibited on the GCE. This indicated the electro-inactivity of Clonazepam on the GC surface. Typical CVs of POLY(DA)-GCE in 0.1 M PBS is shown as Fig. 6B where potential sweep rate of 100 mV/s has been employed. In the absence of clonazepam, no peak appears at the POLY(DA-CS)-GCE. While clonazepam exhibits one redox peak which, the anodic peak potential and peak current for the oxidation of clonazepam at POLY(DA-CS)-GCE is about 0.36713 V and 13.2 μA, respectively. Interestingly, the data obtained clearly show that the combination of POLY(DA) and CS definitely improve the characteristics of clonazepam oxidation. That might be related to the excellent property of POLY(DA-CS), such as high specific surface area and electrical conductivity. The role of POLY(DA-CS) was also important for promoting the electrochemical oxidation of Clonazepam. So POLY(DA-CS)-GCE could be substituted for the oxidation of clonazepam. Fig. 6C shows the CVs of POLY(DA-CS)-GCE in the absence and presence of 0.1 mM Clonazepam. The current response of clonazepam at the POLY(DA-CS)-GCE indicating that this organic hybrid possesses remarkable enhancement effects on the oxidation of clonazepam. It is noteworthy that the present response of Clonazepam was dramatically improved when CS was introduced to the surface of POLY(DA). This can attribute to hydrogen bonding between the POLY(DA-CS) and CLONAZEPAM. This leads to a greater amount of Clonazepam accumulation on the surface of POLY(DA-CS)-GCE. Therefore Clonazepam could be more accumulated on the electrode surface due to their interaction with POLY(DA-CS).

According to the obtained results, the intensity of peak currents obtained by POLY(DA-CS)-GCE is low (13.2 μA). Therefore, we need to use some metals such as gold which no requirement of an external linker or functionalization. Fig. 6D compares the CV responses of a POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE in the absence and presence of Clonazepam. It is observed that a pair of redox peaks of the POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE was appeared, which confirmed electrodeposition of AuNPs on the surface of the POLY(DA-CS). While Clonazepam exhibits one redox peak which, the anodic peak potential and peak current for the oxidation of Clonazepam at POLY(DA-CS)-GCE is about 0.63812 V and 97.4 μA, respectively. This is related to synergetic effect of P(DA-CS) and Au NPs on the signal amplification. We suggest that the redox peaks correspond to the electrocatalytical nature of AuNPs on the surface of the pre-modified electrode. The reason of this approach is low peak current in the absence of Clonazepam by POLY(DA-CS)-Au NPs-GCE (Ipa = 13.2 μA).

In summary, the stepwise assembly of two materials on the GCE is accompanied by a change in the voltammetric response and a variation of the peak to peak separation between the cathodic and anodic waves (ΔE
P ) of the redox probe. An irreversible redox behavior of clonazepam was observed on the modified GCE with a peak-to-peak separation of 0.62 V. Importantly, the response at the POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE was similar to the shape obtained on other modified electrode, and in addition, the redox peak current increased and the ΔE
P decreased with Au NPs assembly. We concluded that this is caused by the Au NPs. First, NPs possess a concentration effect, a directional effect and a quantum size effect. They decrease the resistance of electron transfer and increase the electron transfer rate. Second, Au NPs exhibit relatively good electron conductivity. Third, the high surface-to-volume ratio of the assembled NPs materials may greatly enhance the density of the sensor. These voltammograms showed that the Au NPs were successfully assembled on the POLY(DA-CS) and, as for the electron transfer, it was better than the POLY(DA-CS)-GCE. This may be ascribed to the electrostatic effect of Clonazepam and Au NPs. Based on above discussed electrodes, POLY(DA-CS)-Au NPs-GCE has greater interaction with Clonazepam. Hence, higher peak current of CLONAZEPAM is expected because of existence of Au NPs on the structure of POLY(DA-CS). Therefore, the reason of this phenomena is synergetic effect of POLY(DA), CS, and catalytically role of Au NPs. Similar results were obtained for other drugs.

Scan rate is one of important items affecting electroactivity of various organic molecules. The electrooxidation of selected drugs at various scan rates was investigated on the surface of PDA-CS-Au NPs by CV. The effect of the scan rate on the electrooxidation of selected drugs was studied in the range from 2 to 1000 mV/s using CV method (Fig. 8 ). Two approaches widely used to study the reversibility of reactions and to determine whether a reaction is adsorption or diffusion controlled consist of the analyses of dependences: Ip vs. v 1/2 and lnIp vs. lnv. Inset of Fig. 8 shows these plots for the oxidation peak of selected drugs in 0.1 M PBS.

A dependence of lnIpa on lnv is linear and described by the following equations:
ln
I pa
=
1.002 ln v
V / s
+ 0.4549
mA
R 2
= 0.9971
Clonazepam
ln
I pa
=
1.0298 ln v
V / s
+ 5.7516
mA
R 2
= 0.9887
Diazepam
ln
I pa
=
0.7033 ln v
V / s
+ 8.111
mA
R 2
= 0.9962
Oxazepam
ln
I pa
=
0.3914 ln v
V / s
+ 9.3873
mA
R 2
= 0.9954
Chlordiazepoxide
ln
I pa
=
0.247 ln v
V / s
+ 8.6908
mA
R 2
= 0.9878
Alprazolam

A slope close to 0.5 is expected for diffusion-controlled electrode processes and close to 1.0 for adsorption-controlled processes [42]. Therefore, in the case of Oxazepam, Chlordiazepoxide, and Alprazolam, the oxidation process was diffusion-controlled. But in the case of Clonazepam and Diazepam, the electrooxidation process was controlled by adsorption.

Fig. 9 presents a dependence of Epa on scan rate determined from cyclic voltammogram recorded for the selected drugs electrooxidation. If electrochemical reaction is irreversible, then Epa is independent on v. Thus, it can be concluded that heterogeneous electron transfer in selected drugs electrooxidation is irreversible because Epa increases with an increase in the scan rate. In addition, the value of the overall electron transfer coefficient for the reaction can be obtained from the following equation [43–45]:
(1)
E p
=
RT
2 α F
ln υ + constant
where Ep-peak potential (V), R-universal gas constant (8.314 J K−1 mol−1), F-Faraday constant (96,487 C mol−1), T-Kelvin temperature (298 K), α-anodic transfer coefficient, v-scan rate (V/s).

Using the relation of anodic peak potential on the logarithm of the potential scan rate (Fig. 9), the value of the overall electron transfer coefficient (α n) was obtained as 0.22, 0.63, 0.31, 0.5, 0.18 for electrooxidation of selected drugs (Alprazolam, Chlordiazepoxide, Clonazepam, Diazepam, and Oxazepam, respectively).

The stability of the electrode is also investigated by CV technique based on the number of cycles (Fig. 10 ). To evaluation of electrode stability, CVs performed in 5, 10 and 50 cycles in potential range of −1 to +1 V and scan rate of 100 mV/s in 0.1 M of PBS of selected drug solution (ratio 5:1). According to the results, it is observed that increasing the number of cycles reduce the peak's current. In the applied test to evaluate the stability of the electrode, increasing the number of cycles carried out to 50 cycles. The results obtained from the number of cycles show that by increasing the number of cycles, the peak's current was decreases slowly and in the highest number of cycles (50 and 100 cycles) is observed to be a significant decrease in the current. These results confirm that the electrode has a suitable performance for repeatability of the analysis at least in 50 cycles.

The DPV and SWV are an effective and rapid electroanalytical technique with lower concentration detection limits. The DPV and SWV techniques were employed for the determination of selected drugs in the standard and human plasma samples. Fig. 11 displays the DPV and SWV response of the POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE for increasing selected drugs concentrations under the optimum experimental conditions. As can be seen, one well-defined oxidation peaks were observed for the selected drugs. Meanwhile, a good linear relationship between the peak currents and the selected drugs concentration was obtained (Fig. 12 ). The peak currents were proportional to selected drugs concentration in the various range which was summarized in Table 2 . According to Table 2, the low detection limit of the POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE could be ascribed to the high loading of AuNPs and POLY(DA-CS) by the present method and the rapid electron transfer between selected drugs and the POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE. The analytical results were summarized in the Table 2.

The comparison of POLY(DA-CS)-AuNPs-GCE with other modified electrodes for the detection of selected drugs is shown in Table 3 [45,46]. From Table 3, it can be seen that this method had the comparable sensitivity, low limit of quantification and suitable linear range. Also, the simple electrode preparation procedure is another advantages of this method. Thus, this method allowed to sensitive determination of selected drugs. The prepared electrode shows voltammetric responses with low detection limit for selected electroactive drugs in optimal conditions, which makes it suitable for determination of these drugs. It is found that, by incorporating Au NPs as electrocatalyst center in poly(DA-CS), a novel strategy for developing an efficient and robust electrochemical sensing platform was established. The electrochemical sensor showed high sensitivity and simplicity for detection of some benzodiazepines.

For detection of selected drugs in human serum sample, different concentrations of selected drugs were applied by the standard addition method and obtained results are given in Fig. S1 and Table S1. Therefore, this method could be further developed for the clinical assay of intracorporeal selected drugs content.

The high sensitivity of the proposed method and high surface area of electrode allows the determination of selected drugs in spiked human serum samples. The recovery of the analytes was measured by spiked drugs into highly diluted serum samples. The DPVs were recorded after the serum was spiked with various amounts of the selected drugs within the working concentration range. Recoveries were found to lie in the range of 92.5–110% (Table 4 ).

We have successfully designed a new platform based on Au NPs and POLY(DA-CS) for sensitive voltammetric detection of some benzodiazepines affecting sleep disorders in human serum samples. POLY(DA-CS)-Au NPs film were prepared by the full electrochemically method onto the surface of GCE using layer-by-layer strategy. Au NPs were covalently bonded to POLY(DA-CS) films and the nanocomposite used to entrapment and subsequently detection of selected drugs. The novel detection scheme is a suitable electrochemical assay, which makes the selected drugs detection simple and rapid. The engineered electrochemical sensor showed a suitable linear dynamic range and excellent low limit of quantification. The proposed sensor showed excellent analytical performance for the detection of five benzodiazepines with high sensitivity and reproducibility. The prepared sensor detected selected drugs in unprocessed human serum samples with high recovery values. We conclude that this platform has the potential to be used in clinical applications and real time monitoring of drugs in biological fluids.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work reports a part of PhD by research project of H. Ashrafi. We thank Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for supporting of this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary material

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2018.09.017.
